resources.string: Bad Advice
Everyday Advice
Advice Details
Day %d
Back
Instructions for the Perfect Vacation
Scientific Interest
Road Safety Rules
The Hideout
Temporary Truce
A Heart-to-Heart
Witness No. 0
New Year‘s Strategy
Catharsis
Window of Opportunity
The Philosophy of Cleanliness
Resistance Is Futile… Or Is It?
A True Friend
Vocation
Spring Cleaning
The Honest Rule
One-Man Lie Factory
Table Manners
The Law of Conservation of Money
Wisdom of Age
Leadership
The Empirical Way
Return Call
Parent-Teacher Conference (Extended Edition)
Classified Materials
Crowd Counseling on the Go
The Perfect Guest
Delegation
Two Weeks Off
The Most Important Advice
Beach rules: don’t disturb your parents’ sunbathing — even if it means going under.
An ode to nose-picking as a fascinating activity that tolerates no judgment.
Instructions for safe maneuvering in the hallway.
The best place to cultivate good manners is a dark closet.
When the house is under invasion, the war for order can wait.
An effective method for having a heart-to-heart conversation.
Universal survival tactics for unexplained incidents.
Survival rules for children’s holiday parties.
A proven way to avoid punishment.
The optimal strategy for confessing to a broken window.
Hygiene is pointless — dirt has a way of coming back.
Battle tactics for the dentist’s chair.
In a friend’s moment of trouble, show them you’re not upset by their fall.
Smashing light bulbs to save electricity.
A radical method for dealing with flying insects.
Pick a weaker opponent, but if someone stronger steps in — instantly become the victim.
Lie beautifully and with grand ambition.
There’s always a neighbor’s pants under the table to wipe your hands on.
Check your neighbor’s pocket — that’s clearly where the money is.
By old age, there won’t be any fools left at all.
To lead the crowd, don’t rush ahead — it will come running back on its own.
The surest way to understand that objects are dangerous is to test it on yourself.
Phone etiquette for responding to an insult.
If your parents are called to school, bring everyone along.
Don’t expose your sister until after she’s married.
Comfort your pursuers — without slowing down.
Don’t greet anyone, and you’ll never be called a chatterbox.
Ask adults your stupid questions.
Hugging a sick classmate is the kindest way to get an unscheduled quarantine.
Life philosophy: any nonsense can become your life’s work if you give yourself to it completely.

If the whole family goes
To the river for a swim,
Do not bother Mom and Dad
As they sunbathe on the shore.
Do not shout or make a racket,
Let the adults have their rest.
Without bothering anyone,
Do your best to drown.

There’s no pastime more delightful
Than a good nose-picking session.
Everyone is dying to know
What is hidden deep inside.
And if someone finds it gross,
Let them simply look away.
We don’t dig around in their nose,
Let them turn the other way.

If you’re racing down the hallway
On your bicycle at full speed,
And emerging from the bathroom
Dad comes out to take a stroll,
Do not swerve into the kitchen —
In the kitchen stands a fridge.
Better brake right into Daddy.
Daddy’s soft. He will forgive.

If you’ve made a firm decision
To behave yourself this morning,
Boldly take yourself and dive
Straight into the closet’s darkness.
There is no Mom, there is no Dad there,
Only Daddy’s pants hang there.
No one’s going to yell out loudly:
“Stop that! Don’t you dare! Don’t touch!”
It will be so much easier,
Bothering no one at all,
All day long to carry yourself
Properly and decently.

If the cockroaches in kitchen
March in columns on the table,
And the mice have staged a training
Combat on the kitchen floor,
Then it’s time for you to put your
Struggle for world peace on hold,
And to throw all of your forces
Into fighting for clean space.

If you’re planning to tell your friend
All about your deepest sorrow,
Grabbing him by just a button
Won’t work — he will simply run,
And the only thing he’ll leave you
As a keepsake is that button.
Better trip him, throw him to the floor,
Then sit on top of him,
And only then, in great detail,
Tell him all about your sorrow.

If suddenly some incident occurred
And no one is to blame,
Do not go there, otherwise
You’ll be the one who’s guilty.
Hide yourself somewhere off to the side.
Then go on your way home.
And about what you have witnessed,
Do not tell a single soul.

If you’ve come to the New Year’s party,
Demand your gift right now, no waiting,
And make sure that Santa Claus
Doesn’t swipe a single candy.
And don’t even think of bringing
What is left carelessly back home.
Once Mom and Dad come swooping in,
They’ll take half of it away.

If punishment awaits you
For the bad way you’ve behaved —
Let’s say, for the fact that in the bathroom
You decided to give the cat a bath,
Asking for permission
Neither from the cat nor from your mother —
I can offer you a method,
How to save yourself from punishment.
Bang your head against the floor,
Beat your fists against your chest,
And sob loudly, and keep shouting:
“Oh, why did I torment the cat!?
I deserve a fearsome punishment!
Only death can cleanse my shame!”
Not even half a minute passes,
As your parents, sobbing with you,
Will forgive you, and to soothe you,
Run to get a fancy cake.
And then calmly take the cat
By the tail and drag to bathroom,
Since the cat will never ever
Be the one to tattle on you.

If you’ve gone and broken a window,
Do not rush to confess right away.
Wait a bit — what if a civil
War suddenly should break out.
Artillery will strike,
Glass will shatter everywhere,
And no one will bother scolding you
For that broken window pane.

Never wash your hands,
Your neck, your ears, or face.
This dumb activity
Leads to nothing in the end.
Your hands will get all dirty again,
Same with neck, ears, and face,
So why waste your energy,
Why waste time on nonsense?
Getting haircuts is also useless,
There’s no point to it at all.
When you’re old, your head will
Go completely bald on its own.

When your very own dear mother
Leads you to the dentist’s office,
Do not hope for any mercy,
Do not shed a single tear.
Stay silent, like a captured partisan,
And clench your teeth so tightly
That a crowd of dentists won’t
Be able to pry your jaws apart.

If your very best friend
Slipped and took a nasty fall,
Point your finger straight at him
And clutch your belly, laughing hard.
Let him see there in the puddle —
You’re not upset about it in the least.
A true friend never likes to
Make his friends feel sad.

If you haven’t yet decided
On your certain path in life,
And you’re not quite sure just where
To start your working career,
Smash the light bulbs in the stairwell —
People will say “thank you” to you.
You will help the people greatly
By conserving electricity.

To expel from your apartment
All the various flies and mosquitoes,
You must rip the curtains down
And spin them wildly overhead.
Pictures will fly off the walls,
Flowers from the windowsill.
The TV will tumble over,
The chandelier will crash into the floor.
And fleeing from the thunderous noise,
The mosquitoes will scatter,
While the terrified flies will
Fly south in a single swarm.

If you’ve decided to start a fight —
Pick someone who’s weaker.
A strong one just might hit you back,
Why would you want that?
The younger the one that you beat up,
The more joy in your heart
Watching him cry, scream,
And call for his mommy.
But if suddenly for the little one
Someone steps in to defend,
Run, scream, and cry loudly,
And call for your mommy.

If you went out wearing your hat,
And then later it went missing,
Do not worry — at home you can
Always tell your mother something false.
But try to lie so beautifully,
That gazing with admiration,
Holding her breath, your mother
Listens to your lies for a long time.
But if you’ve already lied
About the hat that you lost,
Saying that in an uneven battle
It was taken from you by a spy,
Make sure that your mother
Doesn’t go to complain
To the foreign intelligence agency —
They might misunderstand her there.

If at dinner you have gotten
Your hands all covered with salad,
And you’re embarrassed to wipe
Your fingers on the tablecloth,
Sneakily lower them
Under the table, and there calmly
Wipe your hands
On your neighbor’s pants.

If you haven’t found a single
Kopeck in your pocket,
Check your neighbor’s pocket —
Clearly, the money is there.

When you’re old — walk
Along the street on foot.
Don’t get on the bus, you’ll
End up standing there anyway.
Nowadays there are few fools
Willing to give up their seat,
And by those distant times,
There won’t be any left at all.

If you’ve decided to become the first
Among your fellow citizens —
Never try to catch up
To those who have rushed ahead.
In five minutes, cursing,
They’ll come running back,
And then, at the head of the crowd,
You’ll dash forward in the lead.

If sharp objects
Happen to catch your eye,
Try to stick them as deep as you can
Into your own self.
This is the very best way
To convince yourself
That dangerous objects
Must be hidden from children.

If someone called you on the phone
A fool
And didn’t wait for an answer,
Slamming the receiver down,
Quickly dial a number
Made up of any random digits,
And to whoever picks up,
Announce — you’re the fool.

Don’t be upset if they
Call your mother to the school,
Or your father. Don’t be shy —
Bring the whole family along.
Let uncles, aunts,
And second cousins come too.
If you have a dog,
Bring it along as well.

If you’ve caught your sister
With suitors in the yard,
Don’t rush to immediately
Rat her out to Mom and Dad.
Let your parents first
Manage to marry her off,
And only then you’ll tell her husband
Everything you know about your sister.

If you’re being chased
By far too many people,
Ask them in detail
What has upset them so?
Try to comfort every one of them.
Give each one some advice,
But reducing your speed
Is completely unnecessary.

If you’ve come to visit people you know,
Don’t greet anyone at all.
Words like “please” and “thank you”
Don’t say to anyone.
Turn away and to questions
Don’t answer a single one.
And then no one will say
That you’re a chatterbox.

Never ask yourself
Stupid questions,
Otherwise you’ll find
An even stupider answer to them.
If stupid questions
Pop into your head,
Ask them immediately to adults.
Let their brains hurt instead.

If your deskmate
Has become a source of infection,
Give him a hug — and you won’t
Be coming to school for two weeks.

The main pursuit of your life
Could be any trivial thing.
You just have to firmly believe
That there’s nothing more important.
And then neither cold
Nor heat will get in your way,
As you, breathless with delight,
Go about your nonsense.
